candidate_problems.text: How can 'capacity for self-corrective representation' be operationalized without collapsing back into surface-performance proxies? The conjecture proposes loop quality, reflexive robustness, and self-criticism as the real measure of epistemic progress, but it leaves unresolved what observable tests distinguish genuine self-revision from persuasive metacognitive performance or post hoc rationalization.

Is self-referential recursion actually necessary or sufficient for epistemic progress? The conjecture treats strange-loop capacity as central, but an open question is whether systems can make substantial epistemic progress through external criticism, modular error-correction, or non-self-modeling mechanisms, and conversely whether rich self-models can exist without yielding better truth-tracking or corrigibility.

What structural conditions allow a system to 'preserve identity through correction' rather than fragmenting into patchwork updates or requiring global retraining? This raises an unresolved problem about the architecture of knowledge-bearing systems: how local revisions propagate, how coherence is maintained across levels of representation, and what tradeoffs exist between stability, plasticity, and corrigibility in self-revising systems.
